text:
Epilepsy Centre

Neurosciences Division


From: Dr Priya Shah (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Sent: 21 September 2025 10:32
To: adult.epilepsy@nhs.net
Cc: Dr Michael O’Connor (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST); Dr Helen Davies (KING'S COLLEGE HOSPITAL NHS FOUNDATION TRUST)
Subject: Transition review and medication optimisation

Hi Priya

Epilepsy Diagnosis:

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy

Background and Transition Notes:

This is the first adult clinic review following transition from paediatric services. The patient attended with a parent, who provided collateral history regarding early seizure semiology and recent fluctuations in frequency. Engagement with adult services has been good, though there has been intermittent difficulty with morning doses on college days due to travel. Parent corroborates patterns of brief staring spells and right‑sided sensory auras since childhood, as well as more recent generalised events. Some text messages from school records were brought in, although a few entries appeared garbled (Ã‚Â·, ï¿½) where dates were exported from an old system.

Present Medication:

1. Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
2. Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily
3. Buccal midazolam 10 mg prn for prolonged events (not used in the last 12 months)

Seizure Phenomenology (parental collateral when present):

- Generalised: Myoclonic jerks upon awakening several mornings per week, lasting seconds; occasional generalised tonic–clonic seizures in the past year (two documented by paediatric team). 
- Focal: Brief right perioral and hand paraesthesiae with preserved awareness, sometimes progressing to impaired awareness with behavioural arrest and lip‑smacking (30–60 seconds). 
- Atonic/“drop” events: sudden knee‑buckling episodes with rapid recovery; helmet not currently used. Records note some entries displayed odd symbols (Ã©, â€, ï¿½) when transferred; timings were clarified verbally in clinic.

Present Seizure Frequency:

Drop attacks are now reported six times weekly, having previously occurred only sporadically, once every few weeks. Parent has witnessed three of these in the past 10 days. Myoclonic clusters occur 3–4 mornings per week; focal sensory auras 1–2 times per week, occasionally preceding a brief impaired‑awareness episode.

Recent Triggers/Context:

Poor sleep around exam periods and missed breakfast appear to precede morning myoclonus. No alcohol or illicit drug use. Stress at college has increased. No intercurrent illness. Adherence reported as good, though two evening doses of levetiracetam were missed last month; no clear temporal correlation with the drop events. Menses do not appear to influence frequency.

Investigations (from paediatric records and adult review):

- EEGs: Generalised polyspike‑and‑wave on wakefulness; intermittent left temporal slowing with rare focal spikes (combined pattern). 
- MRI brain (3T, 2023): No focal cortical dysplasia identified; minor non‑specific T2 foci.
- Vitamin D borderline low (corrected); full blood count, U&Es, LFTs within normal limits (June 2025).

Plan of Action:

1) Diagnosis and classification: Findings remain consistent with combined generalised and focal epilepsy, with both generalised discharges and focal features. The current concern is escalation of atonic “drop” events to six per week. 

2) Medication optimisation: 
   - Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. 
   - Increase levetiracetam to 1,250 mg twice daily for 2 weeks, then 1,500 mg twice daily if tolerated (watch for mood changes, irritability). If behavioural adverse effects emerge, we will reconsider and step back to the previous dose. 
   - Consider add‑on sodium valproate if drop attacks persist despite the above, given efficacy in both generalised and focal seizures; this will be discussed in detail at next review, including risks and shared decision‑making. 

3) Safety: Provided written guidance on head‑injury risk related to drop attacks; advised use of backpack padding for now and to consider a soft protective helmet if falls continue. Midazolam rescue plan reviewed. Driving regulations reinforced (not currently driving). 

4) Lifestyle and adherence: Emphasised sleep regularity, breakfast prior to morning commute, and using phone alarms for dosing. Parent will help supervise evening doses when present. 

5) Monitoring: Arrange ambulatory EEG if drop frequency remains ≥4/week after dose increase, to characterise burden and any focal onset. Repeat vitamin D and bone health advice provided (Ã‚Â· note printed leaflet contained a few odd characters due to formatting, but content explained). 

6) Follow‑up: Nurse telephone check‑in in 3 weeks after levetiracetam titration; face‑to‑face review in 10–12 weeks. Earlier contact if falls escalate, injuries occur, or mood changes develop.

With best wishes

entities.text: Drop attacks are now reported six times weekly
Drop attacks are now reported six times weekly, having previously occurred only sporadically, once every few weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies: (1) Drop attacks: 'reported six times weekly' → 6 per week. (2) Myoclonic clusters: '3–4 mornings per week' → 3 to 4 per week as clusters, but clusters imply multiple events; however, exact per-cluster count not provided, so cannot convert to per time unit precisely. (3) Focal sensory auras: '1–2 times per week' → 1 to 2 per week. Apply rule to select the highest frequency. The highest explicit numeric frequency is drop attacks at 6 per week. No indication of seizure freedom. Therefore, final label: 6 per week.
seizure_frequency_number: Drop attacks are now reported six times weekly
reference: Drop attacks are now reported six times weekly, having previously occurred only sporadically, once every few weeks
